reasoning: The Chicago Fed's National Financial Conditions Credit Subindex (NFCICREDIT) has trended consistently downward since peaking in mid-April 2025 (+0.022). By August 1, 2025, it had fallen for 14 straight weeks to -0.048, with weekly changes typically between -0.001 and -0.004. This index smooths credit conditions, meaning trends exhibit strong inertia and rarely reverse abruptly week-to-week relative to other financial indicators. Therefore, in the very short term (e.g., August 10, corresponding to the August 8 data release), the probability of a reversal large enough to immediately exceed the August 1 baseline is extremely low. Over the subsequent horizons, uncertainty increases and the likelihood of cyclical mean reversion starts taking effect. Historically, the index displays half-cycles of roughly 5 to 6 months (e.g., Nov 2024 bottom to Apr 2025 peak). Following this pattern, a bottom could form in the fall, making an increase relative to the August 1 baseline much more plausible by November 2025 (3 months out) and nearing a coin toss by early 2026 (6 months out), fully reflecting standard mean-reversion expectations for medium-to-long horizons.
